title: Trauma and the Body's Courage: The Bravery Required to Feel Again
excerpt: Discover why healing trauma means cultivating the bravery to reconnect with your body’s sensations and emotions.
body:
Introduction: Why Feeling Again Requires Courage

Trauma changes the way our bodies hold and process experience. For many who have endured trauma, therapy alone isn’t enough to unlock the trapped sensations and emotions. This article explores why reconnecting with your body—feeling again—is an act of courage, and how understanding the body’s responses brings you closer to healing. Drawing on the work of pioneers like Bessel van der Kolk, Peter Levine, and Stephen Porges, we’ll unpack the science behind trauma’s imprint and the bravery needed to move beyond numbness.

Trauma’s Imprint on the Body

Trauma isn’t just a psychological event—it’s a physiological one. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal book The Body Keeps the Score, explains how traumatic experiences get encoded in the body’s nervous system, often manifesting as chronic tension, pain, or shutdown.

When the brain perceives a threat, the autonomic nervous system activates survival responses—fight, flight, or freeze. If these responses can’t be completed or discharged, the body holds residual energy and dysregulation. This creates a state where the nervous system remains on high alert or, paradoxically, numbs itself to protect against overwhelming feelings.

Understanding the Freeze Response

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes the freeze response as a common trauma reaction. Rather than active fight or flight, freeze is a shutdown—a survival mechanism when escape feels impossible. This immobilization can lead to dissociation or emotional numbness that persists long after the traumatic event.

"Healing trauma means helping the body complete the unfinished defensive responses it never got to finish." – Peter Levine

The Courage to Reconnect: Why It’s So Hard

Feeling again after trauma requires courage because it means lowering the protective barriers the body has constructed. These barriers developed to shield you from pain or overwhelm. As Deb Dana, a leading expert on polyvagal theory, explains, the nervous system’s primary goal is safety—when it perceives threat, it restricts connection to sensations and feelings as a protective strategy.

Reconnecting with bodily sensations can trigger anxiety, flashbacks, or intense emotions. This understandably feels threatening and can lead to avoidance. Yet, without this reconnection, healing remains stalled.

Polyvagal Theory and Safety

Stephen Porges’ polyvagal theory offers a roadmap to understanding the nervous system’s states: safe/social engagement, fight/flight mobilization, and shutdown/freezing. Trauma often traps individuals in fight/flight or shutdown modes, making it difficult to access the calm, connected state where healing happens.

Building the courage to feel again involves learning to regulate the nervous system—to shift from defensive states back to safety. This requires patience, support, and self-compassion.

Somatic Healing: Body-Based Approaches to Trauma Recovery

Traditional talk therapy often misses the somatic imprint of trauma. Pat Ogden’s sensorimotor psychotherapy integrates body awareness with cognitive processing, supporting clients in noticing bodily signals and gently tracking sensations without being overwhelmed.

Somatic healing modalities focus on helping the nervous system complete defensive responses, discharge stuck energy, and rebuild a sense of safety in the body. Techniques might include breathwork, movement, grounding exercises, and mindful awareness of sensations.

Completing Defensive Responses

Peter Levine’s work illuminates how the body’s natural survival responses are incomplete in trauma and need to be completed to resolve dysregulation. This could mean allowing the body to tremble, shake, or move spontaneously—actions that release pent-up defensive energy.

The Role of Compassion and Self-Leadership

Gabor Maté highlights that trauma survivors often carry deep shame, which blocks their ability to feel safe in their bodies. Healing involves cultivating compassion for oneself and recognizing that survival responses were adaptive at the time.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model complements somatic work by helping individuals develop a compassionate inner leadership, acknowledging protective parts that hold trauma and working to unburden them safely.

The Path Forward: Guided Courage and Patience

Healing trauma is not about rushing to feel everything at once. The bravery required is about taking incremental steps toward reconnecting with the body’s wisdom, supported by techniques that promote nervous system regulation.

Deb Dana offers practical strategies for co-regulation and pacing, emphasizing that the nervous system needs to feel safe before it can tolerate deeper somatic awareness.

  • Start with grounding practices that bring awareness to the present moment.
  • Practice tracking bodily sensations without judgment.
  • Use breath and movement to gently release tension.
  • Seek support from trauma-informed practitioners trained in somatic approaches.

Conclusion: Courage as a Practice

Feeling again after trauma is the ultimate act of bravery because it means facing the body’s stored pain and uncertainty. But this courage is not reckless; it is measured, patient, and nurturing. By honoring the body’s signals and working with the nervous system’s rhythms, trauma survivors can reclaim their vitality and access deeper healing.

"The body’s courage to feel is the gateway to reclaiming life beyond trauma."

Healing trauma is a journey of reconnecting with yourself at the most fundamental level—your body. It’s a path that demands courage, but also offers profound freedom.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: The Cost of Hidden Stress. Wiley.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: Why does trauma cause numbness or disconnection in the body?
faqs.a: Trauma triggers the nervous system’s protective responses, including shutdown or freeze states, which can numb sensations to avoid overwhelming feelings. This is a survival mechanism that can persist long after the trauma.

faqs.q: How can somatic healing help when talk therapy feels stuck?
faqs.a: Somatic healing works directly with the body’s stored sensations and nervous system states, helping complete unfinished survival responses and restore regulation, which talk therapy alone may not address.

faqs.q: What is the role of nervous system regulation in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Regulating the nervous system helps shift from defensive states (fight, flight, freeze) to safety and social engagement, creating a foundation where the body can safely process and release trauma.

faqs.q: How can I begin to feel my body safely after trauma?
faqs.a: Start with gentle grounding techniques, mindful awareness of sensations, and paced breath or movement exercises. Seek guidance from trauma-informed practitioners who understand somatic approaches.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma lives in the body and offers pathways for healing through somatic awareness.

affiliateProducts.title: In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: This book introduces somatic experiencing and the importance of completing the body’s natural trauma responses.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: A practical guide for understanding and applying polyvagal theory to regulate the nervous system in trauma recovery.

metaTitle: Trauma and the Body's Bravery to Feel Again
metaDescription: Explore why healing trauma demands the courage to reconnect with your body’s sensations and nervous system.
author: The Oracle Lover
